title: How Receptive Fields Grow
concept: When you stack local layers, each new layer combines nearby activations from the layer below, so the visible patch of the original image grows layer by layer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A receptive field answers which question?

options.text: Which original pixels can influence one activation
How many epochs a model should train for
Which app icon to use for the lesson

exercises.explain: Yes — before we can talk about growth, we need the core idea: a receptive field is an activation's upstream image patch.

cards.title: Small windows can team up
cards.body: A single 3×3 filter sees only a tiny patch. But when another layer reads from many nearby activations below it, that new layer indirectly sees a bigger piece of the original image.

cards.title: Growth by stacking
cards.body: Receptive fields grow because each later activation combines information from several earlier activations, and each of those activations already came from their own image patches. Layer by layer, the visible region expands.

cards.title: Ripples spreading outward
cards.body: Drop a pebble into water and the ripple expands ring by ring. Receptive field growth is similar: each new layer pushes influence outward a little farther than the layer before it.

cards.title: A tiny numeric example
cards.body: With stride 1, one 3×3 layer sees a 3×3 patch of the original image. Put a second 3×3 layer on top, and a later activation now depends on about a 5×5 patch. Add a third, and it reaches about 7×7. Small filters, wider reach.

cards.title: Growth doesn't require giant filters
cards.body: You do not need one enormous filter to see more context. Stacking ordinary local layers already grows what later activations can feel.

cards.title: Same Filter Size, Wider Reach
cards.body: As layers stack, the effective visible patch gets larger:

left.text:
One activation depends on
a local 3×3 patch
of original pixels

right.text:
A later activation can depend on
about a 7×7 patch —
same small filter, bigger view

exercises.prompt: Which setup gives a later activation the larger receptive field?

options.text: A lesson title with bigger text

exercises.explain: Correct — stacking more local layers grows how much of the original image a later activation can depend on.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Even if each layer is local, stacking them can let later activations depend on pixels farther apart in the original image.
exercises.explain: True — each layer reaches a little farther, so the effective visible patch expands.

exercises.sentence: With stride 1, stacking two 3×3 layers grows the effective view from 3×3 to about ___.
exercises.explain: Right — the second layer reads from neighbors that already each came from 3×3 patches, so the total reach becomes about 5×5.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains receptive field growth?

options.text: Each new layer combines nearby activations below it, so its view of the original image expands
Receptive fields grow only when you rename the layer
A later layer sees less of the image because it has more numbers

exercises.explain: Exactly — growth comes from stacking local views on top of local views.

exercises.prompt: You stack three stride-1 3×3 convolution layers. About how large is the original-image patch that one later activation can depend on?

options.text: Still only 3×3, because filter size never adds up
Exactly the whole image, no matter its size

exercises.explain: Yes — one 3×3 layer gives about 3×3, two give about 5×5, and three give about 7×7. Next, you'll see why CNNs often prefer many small filters over one giant one.
